sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 47, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Current (39,19,39,44). Expected (39,24,39,34) but got (39,19,39,44). Third RIGHT had anomalous behavior: left square blocked, right square moved right instead of left.
- Goal model: Need to find specific configuration. Many same-row + extreme-horizontal combos unvisited.
- Plan: Check ASCII at row 39 to understand wall positions, then try RIGHT again.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 46 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the ASCII at row 39:
Row 39: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOSSSSSBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
- Cols 0-8: YYYYYYYYY (9 Y's)
- Cols 9-13: SSSSS (5 S's) - left square
- Cols 14-18: BBBBB (5 B's) - wall
- Cols 19-23: SSSSS (5 S's) - wait, this is the left square at col 19?
Wait, the current state is (39, 19, 39, 44). So the left square is at col 19 and the right square is at col 44.
Looking at the ASCII:
- Cols 19-23: SSSSS (left square at col 19)
- Cols 24-31: YYYYYYYY (8 Y's)
- Cols 32-37: OOOOOO (6 O's)
- Cols 38-43: OOOOOO (6 O's) - wait, that's 12 O's from 32-43
- Cols 39-43: OOOOO (5 O's)
- Cols 44-48: SSSSS (right square at col 44)
- Cols 49-52: BBBB (4 B's)
- Cols 53-63: OOOOOOOOOOO (11 O's)
Wait, let me recount:
Row 39: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOSSSSSBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
Let me count character by character:
Y(0)Y(1)Y(2)Y(3)Y(4)Y(5)Y(6)Y(7)Y(8)B(9)B(10)B(11)B(12)B(13)B(14)B(15)B(16)B(17)B(18)S(19)S(20)S(21)S(22)S(23)Y(24)Y(25)Y(26)Y(27)Y(28)Y(29)Y(30)Y(31)O(32)O(33)O(34)O(35)O(36)O(37)O(38)O(39)O(40)O(41)O(42)O(43)S(44)S(45)S(46)S(47)S(48)B(49)B(50)B(51)B(52)B(53)O(54)O(55)O(56)O(57)O(58)O(59)O(60)O(61)O(62)O(63)
So the layout is: Y(0-8), B(9-18), S(19-23), Y(24-31), O(32-43), S(44-48), B(49-53), O(54-63). The left square sits at cols 19-23, blocked by walls at 14-18 to the left and open space at 24-31 to the right. The right square occupies cols 44-48, with open space at 32-43 to the left and walls at 49-53 to the right.
When I tried moving RIGHT from (39, 19, 39, 39), the left square couldn't advance because of the wall at col 14, and the right square moved right instead of left—ending up at col 44. This reveals the movement rule: when one square is blocked, the other moves in the opposite direction of the intended move. So RIGHT causes the left square to move right and the right square to move left, but if the left square is blocked, the right square moves right instead.
Now I need to check if the left square can actually move right from col 19. The cells at cols 24-31 are all Y, so there's space for it to move. Let me attempt RIGHT again to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Row 39 structure: Y(0-8), B(9-18), S(19-23)=left square, Y(24-31), O(32-43), S(44-48)=right square, B(49-53), O(54-63). Left square blocked by B-wall at cols 14-18. Third RIGHT caused anomalous behavior.
Plan: Try RIGHT again from (39,19,39,44) to test movement rules.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT from (39,19,39,44)
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blues = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        blues.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
blues = tuple(sorted(blues))
print(f"Current state: {blues}")
print("gameplay_changed:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'))
print("level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Current state: ((39, 19), (39, 44))
gameplay_changed: False
level_completed: False
